content: HIP REPLACEMENT SURGERY - POST-OPERATIVE CARE GUIDELINES. Total hip replacement (THR) restores function and eliminates pain from arthritic or damaged hip joints. Success depends heavily on adherence to precautions, progressive mobilization, and avoiding dislocation. RECOVERY TIMELINE AND MILESTONES. Hip replacement recovery timeline and milestones. Hospital Stay (1-3 days): Day of surgery: Stand with assistance, begin walking with walker. Day 1: Ambulate with walker or crutches, climb stairs if needed for home discharge. Day 2-3: Continue mobility training, discharge planning. Weeks 1-2 (Early Recovery): Pain gradually decreasing but still significant. Walking with walker or crutches. Sleeping on back or non-operative side with pillow between legs. Dependent for lower extremity dressing and bathing. Weeks 3-6 (Active Recovery): Transition to single crutch or cane. Increasing independence with ADLs. Outpatient physical therapy begins. Driving may be possible if left hip and off narcotics (typically 2-4 weeks). Weeks 6-12 (Rehabilitation Phase): Walking without assistive devices in most cases. Hip precautions may be discontinued (surgeon-specific). Return to more normal activities. 3-6 Months: Full recovery for most daily activities. May return to low-impact recreational activities. 6-12 Months: Maximum medical improvement typically achieved. Full return to activities as cleared by surgeon. HIP DISLOCATION PREVENTION. How to prevent hip dislocation after surgery? Dislocation is a serious complication most likely to occur in the first 6-12 weeks. Posterior Approach Precautions (Most Common): Do NOT bend hip past 90 degrees: No low chairs, avoid bringing knee toward chest. Do NOT cross legs: Keep knees and ankles apart, use pillow between legs when sleeping. Do NOT rotate hip inward: Toes should point forward or outward, never inward when weight-bearing. Sleeping: On back or side with abduction pillow for 6 weeks. Environmental Modifications: Raised toilet seat (4-6 inches) to prevent excessive flexion. Shower chair for seated bathing. Remove throw rugs and secure electrical cords. Keep frequently used items at waist height. Long-handled reacher/grabber for picking up items from floor. Activity Precautions: Use log-roll technique for bed mobility. Keep knees apart when turning in bed. Sit in chairs with arms and firm, elevated seats. When getting in car: Back into seat, bring operated leg in first while keeping hip extended. RETURN TO DRIVING. When can I drive after hip replacement? Left Hip Replacement: May drive automatic transmission vehicle once off narcotic pain medication and reflexes return. Typically 1-2 weeks post-surgery if no complications. Must be able to perform emergency braking maneuver safely. Right Hip Replacement: Must wait until able to safely operate gas and brake pedals. Typically 4-6 weeks post-surgery. Must have adequate hip flexion to reach pedals without violating precautions. Must be off all narcotic pain medications. General Requirements: Not taking narcotic pain medications (impairs reaction time). Able to sit comfortably in vehicle without violating hip precautions. Adequate strength to perform emergency maneuvers. Surgeon clearance obtained. HIP PRECAUTIONS AND MOVEMENT RESTRICTIONS. Hip precautions and movement restrictions. Following precautions meticulously is essential to prevent dislocation. Posterior Approach (Standard): No hip flexion beyond 90 degrees for 6-12 weeks. No adduction (crossing midline) for 6-12 weeks. No internal rotation (toes pointing inward when weight-bearing) for 6-12 weeks. Techniques for Safe Movement: Getting Out of Bed: Roll onto non-operative side. Push up to sitting using arms. Slide operated leg off bed while keeping hip extended. Push up to standing from bed or chair arm. Getting Into Car: Back up to seat. Extend operated leg, lower into seat keeping hip extended. Bring legs into car together (or operated leg first). Recline seat back slightly to decrease hip flexion. Duration of Precautions: Posterior approach: 6-12 weeks typically, some surgeons extend to 3 months. Anterior approach: 2-4 weeks typically. Always follow specific surgeon's protocol.
